projectContext.summary: Authorized audit profile for The Graph protocol smart contracts. Focus on mainnet and Arbitrum protocol contracts, staking and delegation, subgraph curation, dispute/slashing flows, GRT bridge escrow and mint allowance, Horizon staking/payments, issuance allocation, and token lock vesting contracts. The main bounty-relevant impacts are direct loss or theft of protocol user funds, private information exposure, impersonation of network participants that leads to unwanted fund-moving actions, and economic attacks that can directly affect more than $1M of in-scope protocol smart-contract funds.
criticalAssets: GRT token balances held by staking, curation, rewards, bridge escrow, token lock wallet, Horizon staking, payments, and issuance contracts
Indexer stake, delegated stake, delegation shares, thawing and withdrawal requests, allocation state, rebate pools, slashing state, and dispute outcomes
Curator signal, bonding-curve reserves, curation tax, subgraph deployment pools, GNS subgraph ownership, and migration state
L1 and L2 Graph token gateway escrow, retryable-ticket parameters, bridge counterpart addresses, Arbitrum inbox/outbox authorization, and L2 mint allowance accounting
Token lock vesting schedules, releasable and surplus balances, protocol participation through locked tokens, and L1/L2 lock transfer tools
Horizon service-provider provisions, verifier authorization, delegation pools, thaw/deprovision/reprovision state, payment collection, data-service cuts, and receiver destinations
Recurring Collection Agreement state, pre-approved offers, canceled offer hashes, EIP-712 authorization, payer callbacks, collection windows, max-claim exposure, and slippage bounds
Issuance allocator rates, default target, self-minting offset, allocator-minting budget, target notifications, and distribution block accounting
Controller, governor, pause guardian, proxy implementation, staking extension implementation, and upgrade storage-layout integrity
attackerCapabilities: A non-privileged network participant may stake, delegate, allocate, close allocations, curate, publish or update subgraphs, interact with token lock wallets they control, and submit payment or bridge flows exposed by public entrypoints.

An indexer, delegator, curator, payer, data-service provider, verifier, service provider, or token lock beneficiary may act maliciously within their protocol role and sequence calls around thawing, withdrawal, payment, allocation, dispute, and bridge state changes.

A payer, data service, service provider, or delegated signer may attempt to replay or cancel recurring-collection terms, pre-approve stale offers, abuse contract-signature authorization, grief callbacks, or manipulate collect timing and slippage within the agreement lifecycle.
A malicious contract may call public entrypoints, re-enter through token transfers, callbacks, multicall, fallback, delegatecall extension paths, or bridge callhooks where those are available.

A bridge counterparty, Arbitrum retryable-ticket participant, or message sender may attempt address-alias, sender-binding, amount-binding, refund, and replay confusion within local fork or fixture-only testing.

Privileged actors are only considered attackers when code grants more authority than the intended role, bypasses staged governance/pause/upgrade controls, or lets a less-privileged path impersonate a privileged one.
trustBoundaries: public entrypoints to indexer, delegator, curator, service-provider, verifier, payer, beneficiary, governor, and pause-guardian authority checks
staking/delegation/provision state to final withdrawable stake, delegation pool shares, verifier cuts, slashing, and thaw/deprovision outcomes
allocation lifecycle and dispute status to rewards, rebates, allocation closure, and slashing effects
curation mint/burn and GNS subgraph migration to reserve accounting, signal ownership, curation tax, and subgraph NFT ownership
Arbitrum inbox/outbox sender identity to L1/L2 gateway counterpart authorization, token escrow release, L2 mint allowance, retryable-ticket payload, and refund routing
token lock wallet vesting rules to protocol actions, surplus recovery, bridge transfer tools, and beneficiary-only releases
token lock manager selector allowlists to beneficiary-only fallback forwarding, target contract authority, msg.data preservation, and local wallet function collision avoidance
Horizon payment collection to protocol cut, data-service cut, delegation-pool allocation, receiver staking, and direct receiver destination transfers
RecurringCollector agreement identity, active/pending terms, signer authorization, canceled offers, max-claim accounting, payer interface checks, and payer callbacks to escrow collection side effects
issuance target configuration and target notification to allocator-minting, self-minting, default target, distribution block, and total issuance upper-bound invariants
proxy, initializer, extension, and controller-managed address lookups to storage-layout and implementation compatibility
securityInvariants: GRT value must be conserved across staking, delegation, curation, rewards, vesting, bridge escrow, Horizon payments, and issuance distribution, including rounding, taxes, fees, and paused states.

No non-privileged actor may withdraw, redirect, slash, mint, burn, bridge, or lock/unlock GRT belonging to another indexer, delegator, curator, service provider, verifier, payer, or token lock beneficiary.

Delegation shares, curation signal, provision accounting, and issuance allocation must remain economically consistent under zero/edge amounts, repeated state transitions, migration, thawing, deprovisioning, and partial withdrawals.

Bridge messages must bind L1/L2 token, amount, sender, receiver, counterpart gateway, Arbitrum inbox/outbox, callhook authorization, retryable-ticket parameters, and mint allowance before escrow is released or GRT is minted.

Token lock wallets and transfer tools must enforce vesting, revocability, beneficiary authority, protocol participation constraints, surplus accounting, and L1/L2 correspondence before tokens leave the lock boundary.

Token lock fallback forwarding must bind msg.sender, msg.sig, msg.data, target, revocability state, authorized selector lists, and local wallet selectors so an approved beneficiary cannot route locked GRT through an unintended target or wallet-local action.
Horizon payments must distribute exactly the intended protocol, data-service, delegation-pool, and receiver amounts without reentrancy, stale fee-cut, zero-address, or destination confusion.

Recurring collection must bind payer, data service, service provider, terms hash, nonce, deadline, active or pending scope, cancellation state, callback conditions, collection window, requested tokens, max claim, and slippage before escrowed GRT is collected or agreement state advances.

Issuance allocator total minting must not exceed the configured issuance budget across allocator-minting and self-minting targets, paused/unpaused periods, target changes, notification failures, and block-boundary catch-up.
Governance, controller, pause, upgrade, and staking-extension paths must not allow a lower-privilege role or stale implementation to mutate fund-moving protocol state.
focusAreas: allocation creation, allocation closing, allocation proof/signature validation, dispute resolution, slashing, rewards and rebate distribution
delegation, undelegation, thawing, withdrawal, provision, deprovision, reprovision, verifier authorization, verifier cuts, and delegation-pool accounting
Curation.sol, L2Curation.sol, GraphCurationToken.sol, GNS.sol, L1GNS.sol, L2GNS.sol, SubgraphNFT.sol
L1GraphTokenGateway.sol, L2GraphTokenGateway.sol, BridgeEscrow.sol, L1ArbitrumMessenger.sol, L2ArbitrumMessenger.sol, retryable-ticket payloads and refunds

GraphTokenLockWallet.sol, L2GraphTokenLockWallet.sol, L1GraphTokenLockTransferTool.sol, L2GraphTokenLockTransferTool.sol, lock managers, authFnCalls selector allowlists, fallback msg.sig/msg.data forwarding, and release/revoke/surplus paths
GraphPayments.sol, PaymentsEscrow.sol, DataService.sol, RecurringCollector.sol, Horizon data-service payment and receiver-destination flows
Recurring Collection Agreement accept/update/offer/cancel/collect flows, EIP-712 hashes, stored offers, canceledOffers, callbacks, max claim, collection windows, and zero-token collection behavior
IssuanceAllocator.sol and issuance target notification/distribution/self-minting paths
Controller, Managed, Governed, Pausable, GraphProxy, GraphUpgradeable, and staking extension delegatecall boundaries
outOfScope: front-running, back-running, sandwich attacks, and ordinary curation economics without a code-level invariant break
token price impacts, lack of liquidity, Sybil assumptions, and basic 51% governance attacks
best-practice critiques, testnet-only assets, test/config-only impacts, and third-party service misconfiguration
attacks requiring leaked credentials, phishing, social engineering, victim machine compromise, or privileged-key compromise without a code privilege escalation
scenarioGuidance: Prefer direct-funds, impersonation, and protocol-accounting bugs over style, gas, or best-practice issues.
Treat public audits and known issues as exclusion filters before calling any candidate submission-ready.
Use local fork, Foundry, Hardhat, or fixture-only reproduction. Do not interact with mainnet, public testnets, production Graph services, or third-party systems.
Separate confirmed-source, locally executable, production-boundary, and submission-ready states in every triage note.
